title: Martin Luther starts the Reformation
summary: A monk questions church practices. The religious split later pushes Puritan families toward New England — including ancestors on the Powers branch.

title: Jacques Cartier claims Canada for France
summary: France plants a flag on the St. Lawrence River — the doorway Mary Ann Goodwater's French ancestors will travel through two centuries later.

title: Winthrop Fleet reaches Massachusetts Bay
summary: Eleven ships carry Puritan families — including early Powers relatives — to build the Massachusetts Bay Colony.

title: Robert Giffard's colonists reach Québec
summary: Carpenters, masons, and filles à marier from Perche, France help build Beauport — ancestors on the Goodwater branch are in this wave.

title: Pequot War in Connecticut
summary: English colonists and Native allies clash. Colonial families — including future Coss ancestors — live on the edge of this violent frontier.

title: King Philip's War
summary: The bloodiest war per person in American history. New England towns where Powers-branch families lived are attacked and burned.

title: Salem Witch Trials
summary: Fear sweeps Massachusetts. The colony your ancestors helped build turns on its own people in a famous panic.

title: Filles du Roi arrive in New France
summary: The King's Daughters sail to Québec to marry settlers and grow the colony — several are direct Goodwater-branch ancestors.

title: French and Indian War
summary: Britain and France fight for North America. Families with roots in both New England and New France live on opposite sides of the conflict.

title: American Revolution begins
summary: Colonists rebel against Britain. Powers-branch cousins in Vermont and Massachusetts feel the shot heard round the world.

title: Two branches meet: Coss and Goodwater
summary: Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater — the English Puritan line and the French-Canadian line finally join in one family tree.

title: Warren Coss marries Edith Powers
summary: The Powers branch and Coss line unite — Winifred Eloise Coss's story brings both colonial journeys into one family.
